In the previous lesson, we covered financial due diligence, which focuses on the company. But now we're going to do the commercial due diligence. That'll cover a few different industry-specific topics, such as drivers, market trends, opportunities and threats. We'll be looking at things like what the competition looks like, as well as what your market share and your particular market looks like. Like financial due diligence, you'll probably have someone doing the bulk of the heavy lifting for you. But maybe you are a specialist in this sector, and that's why you're buying the business, because it's a perfect match for your personal experience and your passion. And if so, that's great. There really is no better way for you to learn the ins and the outs of the business, other than doing this sort of due diligence yourself. And you still might want to lean in and hand some of it off to your team. But if that doesn't describe you, it's probably not going to be your best option. You're going to be interdependent on third-party people helping you do this. It's very important to hire the right consultant for this work. And they'll typically either work for a flat fee, or maybe you giving them some piece of equity in the business in return for their expertise, as well as their continued input in the business. So it's important to make sure that your hired consultant has experience in the sector, even better if they've had some kind of leadership experience as well. Again, if this is your first deal, the ideal situation would be that you have a passion and an experience in this type of business. But if that's not your situation, a hired consultant could be a good option. You also want to get familiar with the current market trends if you're not already familiar. If you're buying an internet marketing company, look at the trends in the space. Maybe acquisition costs are incredibly high right now. Or the FTC has imposed some new restrictions that can limit what you're able to accomplish. Same idea if you're purchasing a retail store. If you're selling clothing, you need to know what's going to catch people's attention. Depending on the sector, make sure to do your due diligence so you can come into the business day one ready to roll. And again, maybe you're partnering with someone who has experience in the sector already. Maybe you're buying that internet marketing company with someone who has run an internet marketing business for 10 or say 20 years. This due diligence is incredibly important because once you identify the full picture, you can start thinking about the opportunities. You also want to look at who your largest competitors are in the area and find out how much market share they have. And when you're looking at the competition, especially in your direct market, you'll want to look at something called the total available market size. The total available market is the total revenue opportunity that is available for your product or service if you achieve 100% of the market share. You can go online and find data needed to find the total available market. Most about every sector or niche has published market data somewhere. You can usually find comparable businesses or stores in your area, look at their sales, and find out what the average customer spends. For example, if you purchase a burger restaurant and the area you service has a population of 200,000 people where the average customer spends $150 per year, you can then calculate a total market area of 200,000 people and multiply it by your average customer spend of $150. So we're looking at a total available market area of $30 million. If the burger restaurant typically has revenues of roughly 300,000, then you're settling at about 1% of the total available market. Your market share is annual revenue divided by total available market. The same idea goes for retail. For example, if you sell men's clothing and you target the upper 30% of the male population in that area, then you can calculate the number of people you target by multiplying the total men's population by 30%. Then by the following same calculation you can see on the screen now, the target population multiplied by the average annual customer spend, that will give you the total available market. When you look at your competition, you'll want to think of ways you can gain market share against them. So you'll look at the direct and indirect competition. Direct competition is any business that essentially is doing the same thing you are, competing for the same dollars. So if you're buying a retail clothing company, your direct competition are other clothing companies. Indirect competition are companies that are competing for the same business, the same dollars, but they don't offer the same product or service. If you are a software company providing customer relation management software, indirect competition might be a data security company. Both of those services are online businesses vying for the same dollars. And if a customer needs one of those, they likely need the other. If you can offer both, you'll put yourself in a better position to compete with your direct and indirect competition. Think about ways you can leverage your business's reputation, any marketing strategies you can utilize, and any ways to expand your market share. Remember, with the businesses we're targeting, a lot of them are owned by older business owners who maybe rely largely on repeat business or word of mouth. They've probably never done any marketing, especially online marketing or sales, and as they get older, looking to exit the business, they're certainly not going to worry about that now. And if you're buying a business that only serves foot traffic, why wouldn't you start selling online? That could be a huge opportunity for you. You can also do the price analysis and see if your prices are higher or lower than the average and adjust them from there. Depending on course of the business's quality and reputation. And you might find that there's an opportunity to raise the prices. Of course, a lot of this depends on the sector and the market you're in. That's why it's so important to have someone who knows the sector assisting you in the commercial due diligence. You also want to analyze the market history. This means looking at what's been going on in that market, how businesses fit in, etc. etc. You'll want to know all that. And this will help you understand the scalability of the business. Don't forget to identify any other missed opportunities, not only marketing strategies and online sales, but things like social media or online and in-person events. You can review the sales funnel too. Look at the follow-up procedures and see if the business is engaging and re-engaging customers. Look at how they're contacting customers or if they're doing it at all. Something else to consider. Does the company use coupons? Are they even effective in that industry? Then there's a lifetime value or LTV. And that's a big driver for a lot of businesses. Remember our burger joint where the average annual customer spent $150? Well, over 10 years, that's $1,500 in sales from one person if they're a repeat customer. Maybe you don't have repeat customers so your LTV is lower. So then you think how you can create repeat customers. What does it take to gain their business? Not only to get them in the door, but to make the sale. Figuring all that out might give you a strong idea of what you'd be willing to spend to get that customer's business. There's a whole lot of can of worms when it comes to this stuff. You'll need to figure out what your average or ideal customer looks like, what we call your avatar. And by this I mean demographics, age, education, marital status, whether or not they have children, all those sort of things. Then you can also do some supplier analysis in order to understand who your suppliers are for the business. And once again, you're most likely going to have someone doing the commercial due diligence for you or with you that's able to help you understand the things that you're looking at. So your due diligence is an important part of understanding the future of the business you're acquiring. Do not leave this up to a third party. Be actively involved. And being part of the commercial due diligence is a key part of that. In our next lesson, we're going to move to legal due diligence. See you then.
